coredata.dc:title: Amorphous germanium-crystalline bismuth films as a promising anode for magnesium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Nankai University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Nankai University.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhonghua
Song, Meijia
Si, Conghui
Cui, Wenrun
Wang, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Magnesium-ion batteries (MIBs) are promising alternatives to lithium-ion batteries due to their safety and high theoretical specific capacity, and the abundance of magnesium reserves. However, their anodes and electrolytes severely restrict the development of MIBs, so alloy-type anodes provide an effective strategy to circumvent the surface passivation issue encountered with Mg metal in conventional electrolytes. Theoretically, a germanium anode can deliver a high specific capacity of 1476 mAh g−1, but hitherto, no experimental reports have described Ge in MIBs. Herein, we experimentally verified that Ge could reversibly react with Mg2+ ions through the design of dual-phase Ge–Bi film electrodes fabricated by magnetron co-sputtering. Notably, a Ge57Bi43 electrode delivered a high specific capacity of 847.5 mAh g−1, owing to the joint alloying reactions of Ge and Bi with Mg, which was much higher than the specific capacity of Bi (around 385 mAh g−1). Moreover, the Ge–Bi anode showed excellent rate performance, good cycling stability, and superior compatibility with conventional electrolytes such as Mg(TFSI)2. More importantly, the Mg storage mechanism of the Ge–Bi anode was unveiled by operando X-ray diffraction, and density functional theory calculations rationalized that the introduction of Bi to form Ge–Bi evidently decreased the defect formation energy and effectively boosted the electrochemical reactivity of Ge with Mg.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnesium-ion batteries
Alloy-type anodes
Operando X-ray diffraction
Density functional theory calculations
Magnetron co-sputtering

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of the sputtered Ge, Bi, and Ge–Bi films. (c) XPS spectra of the sputtered Ge39Bi61 film. (d–i) SEM images of the sputtered Ge, Ge–Bi, and Ge films.

(a) TEM image, (b) corresponding SAED pattern, (c) HRTEM image, and (d, e) enlarged HRTEM images corresponding to “1” and “2” regions in (c) of the Ge39Bi61 film. (f–i) HAADF-STEM and corresponding elemental mapping images of the Ge39Bi61 film.

(a) CV curves at 0.01 ​mV ​s−1, (b) discharge/charge profiles at 50 ​mA ​g−1, and (c) rate performance of the sputtered Bi, Ge–Bi, and Ge films. (d) GITT curve as well as diffusion coefficients of Mg2+ ions during the discharge process with the Ge27Bi73 film. (e) Cycling stability of the sputtered Bi, Ge–Bi, and Ge films.

(a) Operando XRD patterns and (b) contour plot of the Ge27Bi73 electrode during the discharge/charge processes. The discharge (stages 1 and 2)/charge (stages 3 and 4) profile of the Ge27Bi73 electrode at 50 ​mA ​g−1 is also shown for reference (right part).

(a, c) Discharge/charge profiles and (b, d) cycling stability of full cells assembled with the pre-magnesiated Ge39Bi61 anode and Mo6S8 cathode in (a, b) 0.4 ​M APC and 0.5 ​M ​Mg(TFSI)2/diglyme electrolytes.

(a–c) Stable structures for pure Bi, Ge–Bi, and pure Ge, as well as Mg insertion into these three materials. (d) Plot of the defect formation energies for Mg insertion into pure Ge, pure Bi, and Ge–Bi.

head.ce:title: Amorphous germanium-crystalline bismuth films as a promising anode for magnesium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, West Road of Nan Xinzhuang 336, Jinan 250022, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnesium-ion batteries (MIBs) are promising alternatives to lithium-ion batteries due to their safety and high theoretical specific capacity, and the abundance of magnesium reserves. However, their anodes and electrolytes severely restrict the development of MIBs, so alloy-type anodes provide an effective strategy to circumvent the surface passivation issue encountered with Mg metal in conventional electrolytes. Theoretically, a germanium anode can deliver a high specific capacity of 1476 mAh g−1, but hitherto, no experimental reports have described Ge in MIBs. Herein, we experimentally verified that Ge could reversibly react with Mg2+ ions through the design of dual-phase Ge–Bi film electrodes fabricated by magnetron co-sputtering. Notably, a Ge57Bi43 electrode delivered a high specific capacity of 847.5 mAh g−1, owing to the joint alloying reactions of Ge and Bi with Mg, which was much higher than the specific capacity of Bi (around 385 mAh g−1). Moreover, the Ge–Bi anode showed excellent rate performance, good cycling stability, and superior compatibility with conventional electrolytes such as Mg(TFSI)2. More importantly, the Mg storage mechanism of the Ge–Bi anode was unveiled by operando X-ray diffraction, and density functional theory calculations rationalized that the introduction of Bi to form Ge–Bi evidently decreased the defect formation energy and effectively boosted the electrochemical reactivity of Ge with Mg.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable magnesium-ion batteries (MIBs) are considered as promising candidates for a reliable alternative to Li-based systems because Mg is abundant and inexpensive, and chemically stable [1–3]. The smooth deposition of Mg metal via electroplating in several ether-based electrolytes has put this alternative energy storage vector into the spotlight [4–6]. Over the past few years, initial key milestones in the research and development of cathodes, anodes, and electrolytes for MIBs have been achieved, including high-temperature and ultra-low-temperature Mg storage.

However, many challenges still limit their further application, including the slow transport kinetics of cathode materials, a strong polarization effect, and so on [7–9]. In addition, conventional electrolytes analogous to those in lithium-ion batteries, such as Mg(ClO4)2 or Mg(BF4)2, or Mg(PF6)2 or Mg(TFSI)2 salts in carbonate or nitrile solvents, are not compatible with Mg metal anodes [10], due to the formation of an insulating passivating film that prevents the reversible plating and stripping of Mg2+ ions [11]. On the other hand, the ether-based electrolytes that allow reversible electrochemical Mg plating and stripping are extremely unstable, air sensitive, and corrosive to current collectors [12,13]. These two intractable issues have long been the roadblocks for developing rechargeable MIBs with high energy density.

In recent years, various types of anode materials that can replace Mg metal have boosted the development of MIBs. For example, spinel Li4Ti5O12 as an Mg-ion insertion-type anode material demonstrates an excellent long-term cycle life [14]. In addition, alloy-type anodes have attracted increasing attention due to their low reaction voltages, high theoretical specific capacities, and potential compatibility with conventional electrolytes [15,16]. Pairing alloy-type anodes with high-voltage cathodes and conventional electrolytes conceptually offers a feasible workaround to construct high-energy-density MIB prototypes [17].

Current research in the area of alloy-type anodes is mostly focused on bismuth- and tin-based materials [17]. For example, BiOF nanosheets fabricated by a solvothermal method show superior cycling stability (capacity retention > 96% after 100 cycles) for Mg storage owing to the space confinement of in situ conversion [18]. Some promising elements with high specific capacities and abundant resources, such as phosphorus, germanium, and silicon, have been limited to the theoretical research level [19–23]. Therefore, expanding the range of candidate elements for Mg ion storage is crucial to the development of alloy-type anodes.

A Ge anode can theoretically react with Mg to form Mg2Ge, delivering a high specific capacity of 1476 mAh g−1 [20]. Unfortunately, the alloying reaction of Mg with an elemental Ge anode was found to be very difficult [24], due to the high barrier for inserting Mg2+ ions into the Ge host lattice [22]. Several strategies have been proposed to boost the electrochemical reactivity of less active anodes (e.g., Sn, Ge, Si) with unfavorable defect formation energies, and to promote the ion-diffusion kinetics of solid electrode materials — for example, decreasing the particle size [19,25,26], doping with foreign elements [22,27], using a phase engineering strategy [28], designing an amorphous structure [23,29], constructing a 3D conductive framework [30], or tuning a hybrid electrolyte of Mg2+ and Li+ ​ions [31]. However, to the best of our knowledge, only a few theoretical results about Ge as a potential anode for MIBs have been reported [20,22,23]. The preliminary experimental exploration of Ge anodes for MIBs still remains a great challenge.

In this work, we developed a novel amorphous Ge-crystalline Bi dual-phase anode for MIBs by a facile magnetron sputtering method. The second phase of Bi was introduced into the amorphous Ge matrix to lower the defect formation energy of Mg2+ ion insertion, which significantly boosted the electrochemical reactivity of the amorphous Ge phase. For the first time, we experimentally verified that this unique electrode design enabled the occurrence of reversible alloying/dealloying reactions of Ge with Mg2+ ions. The Ge27Bi73 electrode showed excellent rate performance and good cycling stability towards Mg storage. Based on experimental results and theoretical calculations, we rationalized how the introduction of the Bi phase promoted reversible Mg insertion and extraction in the Ge phase. Furthermore, the Mg storage mechanism of the Ge–Bi anode was unveiled using operando (in situ) X-ray diffraction (XRD). More importantly, the Ge–Bi anode exhibited good compatibility with conventional electrolytes.

Based on the binary alloy diagram and parameter regulations (Fig. S1) [32], we fabricated a series of flexible, self-supporting, and robust Ge–Bi films with different compositions (Ge27Bi73, Ge39Bi61, Ge57Bi43, Ge66Bi34) by a facile magnetron co-sputtering method (Fig. S2). For comparison, sputtered Ge and Bi films were also prepared. The XRD patterns (Fig. 1 a) show that except for the diffraction peaks from the Cu substrate, no other peaks were detected for the sputtered Ge film, indicating the formation of an amorphous phase. Fig. S3 shows the differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curve of the sputtered Ge film. The exothermic peak located at ∼510 ​°C is due to the crystallization of the amorphous Ge (a-Ge) [33]. The diffraction peaks of the Bi phase are easily observed in the sputtered Ge–Bi films, suggesting the formation of amorphous Ge-crystalline Bi (c-Bi) composites. The diffraction peaks of the Ge–Bi films were found to be broader than those of the pure Bi film, and their intensities gradually strengthened with increasing Bi content.

In addition, the sputtered films were investigated by Raman analyses. Two sharp Raman peaks centered at 68.5 and 93.6 ​cm−1 are associated with metallic Bi [34], while the characteristic peaks located at 85.3 and 288.2 ​cm−1 are ascribed to amorphous Ge [35] (Fig. 1b). Two minor Raman peaks at around 120 and 310 ​cm−1, associated with Bi2O3, were observed for the sputtered Bi film. Moreover, the Raman peaks of Bi significantly strengthened with greater Bi content in the sputtered Ge–Bi films. The Raman results also confirm the formation of amorphous Ge-crystalline Bi in the sputtered Ge–Bi films.

Fig. 1c shows the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results of the sputtered Ge39Bi61 film. The peaks located at 162.3/157.0 ​eV and 121.0 ​eV are ascribed to metallic Bi (Bi0) and Ge (Ge0), respectively, and the peaks at 164.1/158.8 ​eV and 125.0 ​eV belong to oxidized Bi (Bi3+) and Ge (Ge4+), respectively, due to the inevitable oxidation of the samples in air. The valence states of Bi and Ge in the Ge39Bi61 film are consistent with those of the pure Bi and Ge films, respectively (Fig. S4).

The low-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Figs. S5 and S6) reveal that all the sputtered Ge, Bi, and Ge–Bi films were composed of large, columnar grains that grew perpendicular to the substrate surface. The cross-sectional SEM images show that the thicknesses of the sputtered Ge, Bi, and Ge–Bi films ranged from 531 ​nm to 1.51 ​μm (Fig. S6). The high-resolution SEM images (Figs. 1d–i) show the morphological details of the columnar grains in the sputtered films. The surface structures of the Ge66Bi34 and Ge57Bi43 films (Figs. 1e and f) were similar to that of the Ge film, which was featureless (Fig. 1d). The surfaces of the Ge39Bi61 and Ge27Bi73 films were comprised of small particles with hundreds of nm in size (Figs. 1g and h). In comparison, the Bi film was composed of irregular stacked blocks (Fig. 1i).

The actual compositions of the sputtered Ge–Bi films were determined by energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analyses (Fig. S7). The elemental mapping images indicate the homogeneous distribution of both Ge and Bi in the sputtered Ge–Bi alloy films (Fig. S8). Additionally, a nanoporous Ge–Bi alloy (43.8 ​at.% Ge, 56.2 ​at.% Bi) was fabricated by selective etching, and it consisted of crystalline Ge and Bi phases (Fig. S9).

The Ge39Bi61 film was selected for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses. Fig. 2 a shows a bright-field TEM image of one particle in the Ge39Bi61 film. The corresponding selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 2b exhibits polycrystalline rings, which match the (012), (104), and (015) reflections of the c-Bi phase. Notably, the overlapping diffuse halo is a typical characteristic of the amorphous phase, verifying the existence of the a-Ge phase in the sputtered Ge39Bi61 film. Moreover, both the amorphous and the crystalline regions can be discerned in the high-resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2c). As shown in the enlarged images, the amorphous region displays a typical maze-like atom distribution, corresponding to the a-Ge phase (Fig. 2d). In contrast, the crystalline region exhibits lattice fringes associated with the (012) plane of c-Bi (Fig. 2e). Scanning Transmission Electron Microscope (STEM) was also utilized to characterize the microstructure of the Ge39Bi61 film using the high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) mode. Fig. 2f shows a HAADF-STEM image of the Ge39Bi61 film. The corresponding elemental mapping spectra manifest the homogeneous distribution of both Ge and Bi in the Ge39Bi61 film (Figs. 2g–i). The above TEM results further confirm the formation of the a-Ge and c-Bi phases in the sputtered Ge–Bi films.

Fig. 3 a shows the cyclic voltammograms (CV) of the sputtered Ge, Ge–Bi, and Bi electrodes in a 0.4 ​M all-phenyl complex (APC) electrolyte at a scan rate of 0.01 ​mV ​s−1. No redox peaks with the characteristics of Mg ion insertion or extraction are observed for the Ge electrode, and only one pair of sharp redox peaks is identified for the Bi electrode. Notably, all the Ge–Bi electrodes display two pairs of obvious redox peaks. One pair of sharp redox peaks is located at similar potentials (about 0.2 and 0.35 ​V vs. Mg2+/Mg) to those of the Bi electrode, corresponding to the alloying and dealloying reactions of Bi with Mg2+ ions. The other pair of broad redox peaks, at around 0.1 and 0.44 ​V vs. Mg2+/Mg, could be attributed to the magnesiation and demagnesiation of the a-Ge phase. Among them, the cathodic peaks at 0.2 and 0.1 ​V vs. Mg2+/Mg are associated with the alloying of Mg with Bi and Ge to form Mg3Bi2 and Mg2Ge, respectively, while the anodic peaks at 0.35 and 0.44 ​V vs. Mg2+/Mg are related to Mg dealloying from the Mg3Bi2 and Mg2Ge, respectively.

Fig. 3b shows the galvanostatic discharge/charge curves of the sputtered Ge, Ge–Bi, and Bi electrodes at a current density of 50 ​mA ​g−1. The Ge electrode showed no electrochemical reactivity for Mg storage. However, introducing an appropriate amount of Bi (> 34 ​at.%) significantly boosted the alloying reaction of Ge with Mg2+ ions. The Ge57Bi43, Ge39Bi61, and Ge27Bi73 electrodes delivered high discharge specific capacities of 847.5, 816.6, and 690.4 mAh g−1 for magnesiation respectively, which were much larger than the discharge capacity of the Bi electrode (around 385 mAh g−1). One pair of obvious discharge (0.22–0.26 ​V vs. Mg2+/Mg) and charge (0.32–0.35 ​V vs. Mg2+/Mg) plateaus are associated with the alloying/dealloying reactions of Bi with Mg2+ ions. In contrast, the other pair of sloping discharge and charge plateaus are attributed to the alloying/dealloying reactions of Ge with Mg2+ ions. The galvanostatic discharge/charge results of the Ge, Ge–Bi, and Bi electrodes are consistent with the CV results, demonstrating that the Bi phase effectively triggered the electrochemical reactivity of the a-Ge phase with Mg in the Ge–Bi electrodes. In addition, the polarization of Ge redox was much larger than that of Bi redox, based on the CV and galvanostatic discharge/charge results, which could be attributed to the intrinsically very low electrochemical reactivity and sluggish reaction kinetics of Ge towards Mg storage, as confirmed by the DFT calculations [20,22,23]. Notably, the nanoporous Ge–Bi electrode showed only one pair of discharge/charge plateaus, related to the magnesiation and demagnesiation of the Bi phase (Fig. S10), implying that the Bi phase could not effectively activate the electrochemical activity of crystalline Ge.

Fig. 3c shows the rate performance of the Ge, Ge–Bi, and Bi electrodes at various current densities from 100 to 2000 ​mA ​g−1. Similar to its behavior at 50 ​mA ​g−1, the Ge electrode exhibited no electrochemical activity at different current densities. The Ge66Bi34 electrode showed weak reactivity with Mg and low discharge capacities. In comparison, the rate performance of the Ge57Bi43, Ge39Bi61, and Ge27Bi73 electrodes improved in sequence, mainly due to the alloy compositions. The Ge27Bi73 electrode showed superior rate capability compared with the Bi electrode, delivering a high discharge capacity of 435.5 mAh g−1 at 2000 ​mA ​g−1. Notably, the low Coulombic efficiencies of the Ge–Bi anodes at low current densities could be attributed to initial side reactions and low reversibility. As the current densities and cycles increased, the side reactions gradually reduced and the active mass of the Ge–Bi anodes that could undergo reversible alloying/dealloying improved, leading to increased Coulombic efficiencies at higher current densities. This phenomenon may be attributed to the hypothesis that high rates emphasize interface-based storage mechanisms and can potentially further suppress the sluggish alloying reaction [36]. The synergetic effect of the amorphous Ge and crystalline Bi phases and the increased phase boundaries of the Ge–Bi films might provide favorable conditions for interface-based storage and promote transport kinetics and Mg storage reversibility in MIBs.

The galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) was used to evaluate the diffusion behavior of Mg2+ ions during the discharge process of the Bi and Ge–Bi electrodes. The average Mg2+ diffusion coefficient of the Ge27Bi73 electrode was calculated to be 3.28 ​× ​10−13 ​cm2 ​s−1 (Fig. 3d), slightly higher than that of the Bi electrode (1.23 ​× ​10−13 ​cm2 ​s−1) (Fig. S11). The higher diffusion of Mg2+ ions contributed to the better rate performance of the Ge27Bi73 electrode compared with the Bi electrode. Fig. 3e shows the cycling stability of the Ge, Ge–Bi, and Bi electrodes at 1000 ​mA ​g−1. The Ge and Ge66Bi34 electrodes both displayed almost no discharge capacity during cycling. The discharge specific capacities of the Ge57Bi43, Ge39Bi61, and Ge27Bi73 electrodes gradually grew until they reached maximum values; this could be attributed to the applied large current density, which required an activation process to promote the complete reaction of active materials. Moreover, the cycling stability of the Ge–Bi electrodes improved with increasing Bi content, and the Ge27Bi73 electrode showed the best cycling performance, which could be attributed to the small volume changes of the Bi phase during the alloying/dealloying processes.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were also carried out to rationalize the electrochemical Mg storage performance of the Ge–Bi electrodes. Fig. S12 shows the Nyquist plots of the Bi and Ge–Bi electrodes after 50 cycles at 1000 ​mA ​g−1. The Ge57Bi43 and Ge39Bi61 electrodes showed larger charge transfer resistance than the Bi and Ge27Bi73 electrodes, which partially accounts for their faster capacity decay. Indeed, the origin of the Ge–Bi films’ capacity decay upon cycling is mainly attributed to the large volume variations arising from the alloying/dealloying processes, with the strong alternating volume expansion and shrinkage causing active materials to detach from the current collector (Fig. S13).

The Mg storage mechanisms of the Ge–Bi film electrodes were investigated via operando XRD (Fig. 4 and Fig. S14). Fig. 4 a shows the operando XRD patterns of the Ge27Bi73 electrode during the discharge/charge processes at 50 ​mA ​g−1. The weak peaks at 24.8° and 29.9° correspond to the Be11Fe phase, which originated from the Be window. (Here, the purity of the Be window was greater than 99 ​wt.%, but it still contained a minor amount of Fe impurity.) With continuous discharge in stage 1, the diffraction peaks belonging to the Bi phase start to weaken and the peaks of hexagonal Mg3Bi2 (JCPDS No. 65-8732) gradually strengthen, indicating that the Bi phase in the Ge27Bi73 electrode firstly reacted with Mg to form Mg3Bi2. When being discharged to the end of the first plateau (stage 1), the crystalline Bi phase completely transformed into Mg3Bi2. In the subsequent discharge in stage 2, a new peak at 23.7° begins to appear and gradually strengthens, which is attributed to the formation of the Mg2Ge phase (JCPDS No. 02-1135). The Mg3Bi2 and Mg2Ge phases can be readily observed in the fully discharged state (end of stage 2). With continuous charge in stage 3, the diffraction peaks of Mg3Bi2 begin to diminish and ultimately disappear, and the peak intensities of the crystalline Bi phase gradually increase. The diffraction peak of Mg2Ge can still be observed at the end of the first charge plateau (stage 3), which suggests that the Mg element was firstly extracted from Mg3Bi2. At charge completion (end of stage 4), the Mg2Ge peak finally vanishes, indicating the dealloying of Mg from Mg2Ge. Only the Bi phase can be identified in the fully charged Ge27Bi73 electrode, implying that the Ge phase was also amorphous. The corresponding contour plot (Fig. 4b) vividly displays the intensity-related phase transformation of the Ge27Bi73 electrode during the discharge/charge processes. The in situ XRD results of the Ge39Bi61 electrode show a similar phase transformation process (Fig. S14).

To confirm the compatibility of the Ge–Bi anode with conventional electrolytes, a Chevrel phase Mo6S8 was selected as the cathode, a pre-magnesiated Ge39Bi61 electrode was used as the anode, and a cathode-limited full cell was assembled with a conventional 0.5 ​M ​Mg(TFSI)2/diglyme electrolyte (Fig. 5 ). For comparison, a full cell with the 0.4 ​M APC electrolyte was also assembled. Fig. 5a shows the stable discharge/charge profiles of the full cell in the APC electrolyte, with two obvious sloping voltage plateaus observable during the discharge process. Similar discharge/charge curves appear for the full cell in the Mg(TFSI)2/diglyme electrolyte (Fig. 5c). The full cell was stably cycled in the APC electrolyte with a discharge capacity of around 60 mAh g−1 (Fig. 5b). In comparison, the full cell with the Mg(TFSI)2/diglyme electrolyte delivered a much higher specific capacity of about 90 mAh g−1 (Fig. 5d). Mg(TFSI)2 as a “simple” non-nucleophilic salt has been widely studied due to its good ionic conductivity and high solubility in ether solvents [37,38], which may be the qualities that made it more conducive to the insertion of Mg2+ ions into the Mo6S8 cathode and Ge–Bi anode. In addition, due to the higher stability of Mg(TFSI)2/diglyme compared to APC, the charge voltage reached up to 2.0 ​V for the full cell, leading to higher specific capacity in the Mg(TFSI)2/diglyme electrolyte. The above results demonstrate that the Ge–Bi anode had excellent compatibility with conventional electrolytes like Mg(TFSI)2 in diglyme for MIBs. However, the specific capacities of the full cells attenuated rapidly after 10 cycles (Fig. S15). The minor amount of water in the electrolyte and the matching of anode/cathode/electrolyte may have a negative influence on the cycling stability of the full cells.

Finally, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to rationalize the excellent Mg storage performance of the Ge–Bi film electrodes (Fig. 6 ). The pure Ge film electrode displays no electrochemical activity for Mg storage, but the introduction of the Bi phase could trigger and significantly enhance the electrochemical reactivity of Ge with Mg in the dual-phase Ge–Bi electrodes (Fig. 3). Figs. 6a–c show the stable structures of pure Bi, Ge–Bi, and pure Ge, with the insertion of Mg in each. For simplicity, crystalline Ge instead of the amorphous phase was utilized for the DFT calculations. Herein, the defect formation energy (E
f ) was calculated. If E
f is positive, the alloying or insertion of Mg is unfavorable for the electrode. This inverse is true for a negative value of E
f [22]. As shown in Fig. 6d, the E
f is 1.527 ​eV for the pure Ge electrode, which is more positive than the E
f of the pure Bi electrode (0.454 ​eV). Notably, the introduction of Bi evidently decreases the E
f of the Ge–Bi electrode (−1.414 ​eV). The negative E
f value indicates the easy insertion of Mg into the Ge–Bi electrode, which is in good agreement with the experimental results. The effective facilitation in the Ge–Bi anodes could be attributed to a scenario in which the increased second phase and phase boundary facilitate Mg diffusion and entry into Ge. Additionally, the Bi phase in Ge–Bi would firstly react with Mg to form Mg3Bi2, based on the operando XRD results (Fig. 4). Mg3Bi2 is a fast Mg2+ transport medium. The calculated Mg diffusivity in α-Mg3Bi2 (3.9 ​× ​10−10 ​cm2 ​s−1) is several orders of magnitude larger than in Bi (5.9 × 10−14 ​cm2 ​s−1) [39], so the former is more conducive to promoting the electrochemical reaction of Ge with Mg.

In summary, a novel Ge–Bi film anode composed of amorphous Ge and crystalline Bi was fabricated for MIBs by a magnetron co-sputtering method. Unlike a pure Ge film, which showed no electrochemical activity for Mg storage, dual-phase Ge–Bi electrodes with variable Ge/Bi ratios exhibited significantly enhanced Mg storage performance. Specially, the Ge57Bi43 electrode delivered a high discharge specific capacity of 847.5 mAh g−1, and the Ge27Bi73 electrode showed excellent rate performance and good cycling stability. Moreover, the Ge–Bi anode showed good compatibility with the conventional electrolyte Mg(TFSI)2 in diglyme. The Mg storage mechanism of the Ge–Bi anode, unveiled by operando XRD, was associated with the reversible alloying/dealloying of Ge–Bi during the discharge/charge process (Ge ​+ ​Bi ↔ Mg3Bi2 ​+ ​Mg2Ge). Combining experimental results with DFT calculations, we confirmed that the introduction of Bi in the Ge–Bi anode effectively boosted the electrochemical reactivity of Ge with Mg, and this was correlated with a lower defect formation energy for Mg2+ ion insertion. Our findings could provide valuable information for the design of high-performance anode materials for MIBs.

The Ge–Bi films were directly co-sputtered onto a Cu foil substrate by a magnetron sputtering apparatus (SKY Technology Development Co., Ltd., China) using high-purity Ge and Bi targets (99.99 ​wt.%). The sputtering was operated at room temperature for 0.5 ​h in an argon atmosphere. Direct current power supplies of 25, 50, and 75 ​W for the Ge target and a direct current power supply of 25 ​W for the Bi target were applied to fabricate the Ge27Bi73, Ge39Bi61, and Ge57Bi43 films, respectively. The Ge66Bi34 film was prepared using a direct current power supply of 75 ​W for Ge and a direct current power supply of 15 ​W for Bi. The sample holder was rotated at 5 ​revolutions per minute to guarantee homogeneous Ge/Bi deposition on the Cu substrate surface. For comparison, the sputtered Ge and Bi films were obtained through the same procedure but using a single Ge or Bi target. Finally, tailored discs with a diameter of 12 ​mm were used directly as the electrodes for half-cell tests.

XRD (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China) equipped with Cu Kα radiation was used to characterize the phase constitutions of the sputtered films. SEM (ZEISS SIGMA300) was used to characterize the morphology of the sputtered films. An EDX analyzer attached to the SEM was used to determine the chemical compositions of the sputtered Ge–Bi films. Raman spectra were collected using a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman system (HORIBA Scientific, λ ​= ​488 ​nm). The elemental valence states were analyzed using XPS (ESCALAB 250) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (150 ​W) at room temperature. TEM (JEM-ARM200F) was performed to characterize the microstructure of the sputtered Ge39Bi61 film.

CR2032 coin cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box, with the sputtered films as the working electrode and freshly polished Mg as both the reference and counter electrodes. Glass fiber (GF/D) from Whatman was used as a separator, and 0.4 ​M APC solution was used as the electrolyte. The 0.4 ​M APC electrolyte was synthesized by reacting 1 ​M aluminum chloride (AlCl3) and 2 ​M phenyl-magnesium-chloride (PhMgCl) in a tetrahydrofuran (THF) solvent. CV tests were conducted using a CHI660C potentiostat at a scan rate of 0.01 ​mV ​s−1 within a potential window of 0–1.0 ​V vs. Mg2+/Mg. Galvanostatic discharge/charge tests (0–1.0 ​V vs. Mg2+/Mg) were performed on a LANDCT2001A instrument (Wuhan, China) at room temperature. EIS tests were performed using a potentiostat (Zennium, Zahner) in a frequency range from 0.01 ​Hz to 100 ​kHz with a 5 ​mV amplitude. The discharged and charged electrodes were at various stages disassembled from the cells and then washed with THF in the glove box for ex situ characterizations. For the operando XRD, the Ge27Bi73 and Ge39Bi61 electrodes were uniformly sputtered onto stainless steel mesh to ensure electrolyte transport. The operando XRD measurements were conducted using CR2032 coin cells on which one side was a beryllium (Be) window (12 ​mm in diameter) for X-ray beam transmission.

DFT calculations of the geometry and energy were performed using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Program (VASP) code. The electronic exchange-correlation interactions were determined with the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) function, and the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) method was used to describe the exchange-correlation potential in all calculations. The Brillouin zone was sampled with the 4 ​× ​4 ​× ​4, 6 ​× ​6 ​× ​2, and 7 ​× ​7 ​× ​2 Monkhorst-Pack k-point mesh for Ge, Bi, and Ge–Bi, and a heterojunction was constructed for the Ge–Bi model, which was then relaxed. The stopping criterion for ionic upd. (EDIFFG) was set at −0.01. A cutoff energy of plane-wave basis was set with an adequate of 400 ​eV, and accurate precision of convergence for the geometry optimization was set to 10−6 ​eV/Å for energy change. The defect formation energy (E
f ) was calculated according to the following equation,
Ef ​= ​Et, doped – Et, undoped – Ebulk, Mg
where E
t, doped and E
t, undoped were the total energies of the system with and without doped Mg, respectively, and E
bulk, Mg was the energy of bulk Mg.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51871133), Taishan Scholar Foundation of Shandong Province, the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province (2021ZLGX01), and the program of Jinan Science and Technology Bureau (2019GXRC001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.esci.2022.07.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prototype systems for rechargeable magnesium batteries
